content.text: Calling the setter function returned by
does not change the current variable — it tells React that the component needs a new render with a new value. The update moves through a five-step cycle before it appears on screen.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing the five-step state update cycle: event fires → setState called → React queues update → re-render → DOM committed

content.text: A user clicks, types, or async work completes.
React records the new value and marks the component as needing a re-render.
Multiple setState calls in the same event handler are grouped into one render pass.
React calls the component again from the top.
now returns the new value from its internal store.
React diffs the new output against the previous one and surgically updates only what changed.
The Two Forms of the Setter
accepts either a direct value or an updater function.
Use the direct form when the new value does not depend on the previous value — you know exactly what state should become.
Use the updater function when the new value depends on the previous one. React applies the function with the
state — not the snapshot from the current render. This avoids stale-closure bugs when multiple updates fire in rapid succession.

Each render creates its own closure — its own snapshot of every variable in scope at that moment, including state. Event handlers capture the state value from the render in which they were created. If React batches two updates in one event handler, both handlers close over the
snapshot, which leads to one update overwriting the other.
The updater form breaks the dependence on the closure. React queues the functions and applies them sequentially: the first function gets
React batches all state updates that occur within the same event handler into a single re-render. This is an optimization — calling three setters in a row still costs one render, not three.
Since React 18, batching also applies to updates inside
, and native event handlers. Before React 18, only React event handlers were batched.
If you ever need to force an update to flush synchronously (rare, mostly in tests), use
and wrap the setter call in
wrapper handles this automatically in tests.
If you call the setter with a value that is identical to the current state (by
comparison), React bails out and skips the re-render entirely:

This bail-out is why you must always create a new object or array when updating state that holds a reference type. Mutating in place keeps the same reference, so React sees no change and the UI does not update.
State Is a Snapshot — Not a Live Reference

A common source of confusion: calling setState does not change the state variable in the current render. The variable is a constant for this render call — it holds the value React gave when it invoked the component function. The new value is only visible in the
If you need to use the new value after calling setState within the same event handler, capture it in a local variable first:
When state is an object, create a new object on every update. The spread operator is the standard shorthand:
For deeply nested objects, each level must be spread. If this becomes unwieldy, consider the
library, which lets you write mutation-style code that is transparently converted to new object creation.
Arrays must also be replaced, not mutated. Use methods that return new arrays rather than those that mutate in place:
arr.map((v, i) => i === idx ? value : v)
Here is a todo list that demonstrates adding, toggling, and removing items — each using immutable update patterns:
Multiple State Updates and Render Count
A useful mental model: React counts how many times the component function runs. One click that calls two setters = one render (batching). Two separate events = two renders.
Async callbacks (fetch, setTimeout, subscriptions) capture the state at the time the callback was created. When the callback fires later, the state it closed over may be stale.
When you need the latest state inside an async callback without relying on the closure, the updater form
gives you the true current value at execution time. Alternatively, store the value in a ref, which is mutable and always holds the latest without causing re-renders.
State Updates and React's Render Phases
React's update pipeline has two phases:

React calls component functions and builds a description of the new UI (the virtual DOM). This phase must be pure — no side effects, no DOM mutations. State updates during the render phase are re-queued, not applied immediately.
React compares the new virtual DOM to the previous one and applies the minimal set of changes to the real DOM. Effects (

In React's Strict Mode (development only), React intentionally double-invokes the render phase to catch accidental side effects. You may see your component function run twice per setState — this is expected and intentional.
Expecting to read the new value immediately after setState.
The current render's variable holds the old value. The new value only appears in the next render.
Calling setState inside the render body unconditionally.
Causes an infinite loop. State updates must be in event handlers or
Mutating objects or arrays in place.
check sees the same reference and skips the re-render.
Using the direct form when the updater is needed.
If the new value depends on the previous value and multiple updates might queue, use
, which flushes all pending state updates and effects before assertions run. For manual
calls or direct state updates in tests, wrap them in
Optimistic Updates: Updating UI Before the Server Responds
A common pattern is to update state immediately when the user acts, then correct it if the server returns an error. This is called an
— you optimistically assume the server will succeed and rollback only on failure.
The user sees an instant response. If the network request fails, two more setState calls roll back both values. Because both rollback calls are in the same
block, React batches them into one re-render.
Before adding a new state variable, ask whether the value can be computed from existing state or props. Storing derived values in state creates two sources of truth that can fall out of sync.
The rule: if a value can be computed from existing state or props on every render at negligible cost, do not store it in state. Reserve
for values that cannot be derived.

When the same component renders with different data (e.g. a chat window showing different conversations), you may need to reset local state when a key prop changes. The idiomatic approach is to give the component a
tied to the identifier — React unmounts and remounts the component, resetting all state:
the render with the stale draft is already visible.
Calling the setter schedules a re-render — it does not change the current render's variable.
when the new value is independent; use the updater form
when it depends on the previous value.
React batches all updates within one event into a single re-render.

Never mutate objects or arrays in state — always produce new values.
Async callbacks capture a stale snapshot; use the updater form or a ref to access the latest state inside them.
